title: The Church-Warden And The Curate
content:
I.
Eh? good daïŋ―y! good daïŋ―y! thaw it beïŋ―nïŋ―t not mooch of a daïŋ―y,
Nasty, casselty (1) weather! anïŋ― mea Haïŋ―fe down wiïŋ― my haïŋ―y! (2)

II.
How be the farm gittin on? noïŋ―ways. Gittin on iïŋ―deeïŋ―d!
Why, tonups was Haïŋ―fe on ïŋ―em fingers anïŋ― toas, (3) anïŋ― the mare brokken-kneeïŋ―d,
Anïŋ― pigs didnïŋ―t sell at fall, (4) anïŋ― wa lost wer Haldeny cow,
Anïŋ― it beïŋ―ts ma to knaw wot she died on, but woolïŋ―s looking oop ony how.

III.
Anïŋ― soïŋ― theyïŋ―ve maïŋ―de tha a parson, anïŋ― thouïŋ―ll git along, niver fear,
Fur I beïŋ―n chuch-warden mysen iïŋ― the parish fur fifteen year.
Wellïŋ―sin ther beïŋ― chuch-wardens, ther mun be parsons anïŋ― all,
Anïŋ― if tïŋ―ïŋ―ne stick alongside tïŋ―uther (5) the chuch weïŋ―nt happen a fall.

IV.
Fur I wur a Baptis wonst, anïŋ― ageïŋ―n the toithe anïŋ― the raïŋ―te,
Till I fun (6) that it warnïŋ―t not the gaïŋ―inist (7) waïŋ―y to the narra Gaïŋ―te.
Anïŋ― I canïŋ―t abeïŋ―r ïŋ―em, I canïŋ―t, fur a lot on ïŋ―em coomïŋ―d ta-year (8)ïŋ―
I wur down wiïŋ― the rheumatis thenïŋ―to my pond to wesh thessens theereïŋ―
Sa I sticks like the ivin (9) as long as I lives to the owd chuch now,
Fur they weshïŋ―d their sins iïŋ― my pond, anïŋ― I doubts they poisonïŋ―d the cow.

V.
Ay, anïŋ― ya seed the Bishop. They sayïŋ―s ïŋ―at he coomïŋ―d fra nowtïŋ―
Burn iïŋ― traïŋ―de. Sa I warrants ïŋ―e niver said haïŋ―fe wot ïŋ―e thowt,
But ïŋ―e creeïŋ―pt anïŋ― ïŋ―e crawlïŋ―d along, till ïŋ―e feeïŋ―ld ïŋ―e could howd ïŋ―is oïŋ―n,
Then ïŋ―e married a greïŋ―t Yerlïŋ―s darter, anïŋ― sits oïŋ― the Bishopïŋ―s throan.

VI.
Now Iïŋ―ll gie the a bit oïŋ― my mind anïŋ― tha weant be taakinïŋ― offence,
Fur thou be a big scholard now wiïŋ― a hoonderd haïŋ―cre oïŋ― senseïŋ―
But sich an obstropulous (10) ladïŋ―naay, naayïŋ―fur I minds tha sa well,
Thaïŋ―d niver not hopple (11) thy tongue, anïŋ― the tongueïŋ―s sit afire oïŋ― Hell,
As I says to my missis to-daïŋ―y, when she hurlïŋ―d a plaïŋ―te at the cat
Anïŋ― anoother ageïŋ―n my noïŋ―se. Ya was niver sa bad as that.

VII.
But I minds when iïŋ― Howlaby beck won daïŋ―y ya was ticklinïŋ― oïŋ― trout,
Anïŋ― keeïŋ―per ïŋ―e seed ya an roonïŋ―d, anïŋ― ïŋ―e bealïŋ―d (12) to ya ïŋ―Lad coom houtïŋ―
Anïŋ― ya stood oop naïŋ―kt iïŋ― the beck, anïŋ― ya tellïŋ―d ïŋ―im to knaw his awn plaïŋ―ce
Anïŋ― ye callïŋ―d ïŋ―im a clown, ya did, anïŋ― ya thrawïŋ―d the fish iïŋ― ïŋ―is faïŋ―ce,
Anïŋ― ïŋ―e tornïŋ―d (13) as red as a stag-tuckeyïŋ―s (14) wattles, but theer anïŋ― then
I coïŋ―mbïŋ―d ïŋ―im down, fur I promised yaïŋ―d niver not do it ageïŋ―n.

VIII.
Anïŋ― I cotchïŋ―d tha wonst iïŋ― my garden, when thou was a height-year-howd, (15)
Anïŋ― I fun thy pockets as full oïŋ― my pippins as iver theyïŋ―d ïŋ―owd, (16)
Anïŋ― thou was as peïŋ―rky (17) as owt, anïŋ― tha maïŋ―de me as mad as mad,
But I says to the ïŋ―keeïŋ―p ïŋ―em, anïŋ― welcomeïŋ― fur thou was the Parsonïŋ―s lad.

IX.
An Parson ïŋ―e ïŋ―ears on it all, anïŋ― then taïŋ―kes kindly to me,
Anïŋ― then I wur chose Chuch-warden anïŋ― coomïŋ―d to the top oïŋ― the tree,
Fur Quolotyïŋ―s hall my friends, anïŋ― they maïŋ―kes ma a help to the poor,
When I gits the plaïŋ―te fuller oïŋ― Soondays nor ony chuch-warden afoor,
Fur if iver thy feytherïŋ―ed riled me I kepïŋ― mysen meeïŋ―k as a lamb,
Anïŋ― saw by the Graïŋ―ce oïŋ― the Lord, Mr. Harry, I ham wot I ham.

X.
But Parson ïŋ―e will speïŋ―k out, saw, now ïŋ―e be sixty-seven,
Heïŋ―ll niver swap Owlby anïŋ― Scratby fur owt but the Kingdom oïŋ― Heaven:
Anïŋ― thouïŋ―II be ïŋ―is Curate ïŋ―ere, but, if iver tha meïŋ―ns to git ïŋ―igher,
The mun tackle the sins oïŋ― the Woïŋ―ld, (18) anïŋ― not the faults oïŋ― the Squire.
Anïŋ― I reckons thaïŋ―ll light of a livinïŋ― some-wheers iïŋ― the Wowd (19) or the Fen,
If tha cottons down to thy betters, anïŋ― keeïŋ―ps thysen to thysen.
But niver not speïŋ―k plaïŋ―in out, if tha wants to git forrards a bit,
But creeïŋ―p along the hedge-bottoms, anïŋ― thouïŋ―ll be a Bishop yit.

XI.
Naïŋ―y, but tha mun speïŋ―k hout to the Baptises here iïŋ― the town,
Fur moïŋ―st on ïŋ―em talks ageïŋ―n tithe, anïŋ― Iïŋ―d like the to preïŋ―ch ïŋ―em down,
Fur theyïŋ―ve bin a-preïŋ―chinïŋ― mea down, they heve, anïŋ― I haïŋ―tes ïŋ―em now,
Fur they leïŋ―ved their nasty sins iïŋ― my pond, anïŋ― it poisonïŋ―d the cow.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
